sections.Categories and Classification of Hyperte: Hypertensive crisis: SBP >179 or DBP >109 mmHg, potentially life-threatening. Hypertensive emergency = severe hypertension WITH new/progressive end-organ damage (brain, heart, kidney) — requires BP reduction within minutes-hours using parenteral agents. Hypertensive urgency = severe hypertension WITHOUT new/worsening end-organ injury — BP lowered over hours-24h with oral agents. The absolute BP level does NOT define emergency vs urgency; rate of rise and prior baseline BP are more important. Accelerated hypertension = retinal haemorrhages/exudates WITHOUT papilloedema (urgency); WITH papilloedema = hypertensive encephalopathy (emergency). 'Malignant hypertension' is a term best avoided (used inconsistently). ACC/AHA 2017: Normal <120/<80; Elevated 120-129/<80; Stage 1 130-139 or 80-89; Stage 2 ≥140 or ≥90 mmHg.
sections.Measurement and Clinical Assessment: BP measurement: cuff bladder width 40% and length 80% of arm circumference; undersized/loose cuff falsely ELEVATES readings. Korotkoff phase 1 (first sound) = systolic; phase 5 (sound disappearance) = diastolic. Automated oscillometric devices are LESS reliable in shock, arrhythmias and mechanical ventilation. Intra-arterial monitoring is continuous/immediate but not automatically 'most accurate'. Neurological assessment: fundoscopy mandatory (cotton wool spots, flame haemorrhages, papilloedema); neuroimaging mandatory for acute intracranial events — typical hypertensive bleeds occur in basal ganglia/thalamus and cerebellum. Cardiovascular assessment: ECG/CXR for suspected ACS, LV dysfunction or dissection; systolic LV dysfunction clues = lower BP, no retinopathy, four-chamber cardiomegaly on CXR; diastolic dysfunction clues = higher BP, significant retinopathy, LVH on ECG, without cardiomegaly. BP should be measured in both arms if dissection is suspected. Renal assessment: FBC/U&E/creatinine, urinalysis, peripheral smear for schistocytes; higher prevalence of secondary hypertension (renal artery stenosis, glomerulonephritis, Cushing's, phaeochromocytoma) in hypertensive crisis.
sections.Emergency Management Principles: Three-question framework: (1) Should BP be lowered acutely? (2) How much? (3) Which medication? Target: reduce MAP by 20-25%, or DBP to 100-110 mmHg, over minutes-hours — NOT to a normal range, since chronic hypertensives have a rightward-shifted cerebral (and renal/coronary) autoregulatory curve; a 'normal' MAP may fall below their perfusion threshold, causing ischaemia. Previously normotensive patients (acute glomerulonephritis, cocaine, preeclampsia) may sustain end-organ injury at DBP as low as 100-110 mmHg. Pathophysiology: acute rise in SVR (catecholamines, α-adrenergic activity, RAAS activation) is the most common mechanism; fluid overload is a less common contributor. Patients with hypertensive crises typically have a REDUCED circulating volume (pressure natriuresis) — diuretics have a limited role except with obvious fluid overload. Urine output is a useful monitor when lowering BP (falling output = don't lower further, especially if renal artery stenosis is possible).
sections.Pharmacotherapy of Hypertensive Crises: IV agents (onset/duration): nitroprusside (immediate/1-2min, cyanide/thiocyanate toxicity risk especially with hepatic/renal impairment, avoid >3mcg/kg/min for >72h, max 10mcg/kg/min for ≤10min, treat cyanide toxicity with 100% O2 + sodium thiosulphate 150mg/kg IV ± nitrite/hydroxycobalamin; avoided in AMI due to coronary steal); labetalol (7:1 β:Ρ ratio IV, minimal HR/CO change, contraindicated in obstructive lung disease and 2nd/3rd degree heart block, safe in preeclampsia, CONTRAINDICATED in phaeochromocytoma crisis); nitroglycerin (coronary/venous dilator, ACS); nicardipine (dihydropyridine CCB, minimal negative inotropy, good in preeclampsia); fenoldopam (DA1 agonist, renal vasodilation + natriuresis, no toxic metabolites, good in renal impairment); esmolol (cardioselective, perioperative); phentolamine (α-blocker, catecholamine excess, drug of choice in phaeochromocytoma); urapidil (α1-blocker + central 5-HT1A agonism, no reflex tachycardia); enalaprilat (IV ACEi, contraindicated in pregnancy, caution in renal artery stenosis); hydralazine (direct arteriolar dilator, causes reflex tachycardia — avoid in ischaemia/dissection). Oral urgency agents: labetalol, clonidine (central α2-agonist, avoid if sedation would impair neuro assessment), ACE inhibitors (avoid if renal artery stenosis suspected). Sublingual/bite-and-swallow fast-acting nifedipine is UNACCEPTABLE — uncontrollable rate/magnitude of BP drop, associated with serious adverse events including death.
sections.Management of Specific Conditions: Neuro: Hypertensive encephalopathy: diagnosis of exclusion (cerebral oedema on CT without haemorrhage/infarction); reduce BP 10-15% in first hour, <25% over first few hours, using labetalol/esmolol; AVOID clonidine (sedation confounds neuro assessment) and other sedating agents; mental status should improve within hours — if not, reconsider diagnosis. PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome): associated with hypertensive encephalopathy, preeclampsia/eclampsia, immunosuppressants, erythropoietin; potentially fully reversible. Acute intracranial events: injured brain loses autoregulation and becomes pressure-dependent for collateral flow — lowering BP can worsen ischaemia. Ischaemic stroke eligible for thrombolysis: target SBP <185, DBP <110 mmHg. NOT eligible: treat only if SBP >220 or DBP >120 mmHg. Labetalol/nicardipine recommended; nitroprusside/nitroglycerin can increase cerebral blood volume and ICP; nicardipine causes cerebral vasodilation and increased ICP (little advantage over direct vasodilators); labetalol does not appear to increase ICP. ICH: presenting SBP 150-220 mmHg may warrant reduction; ICP monitoring may guide targets via cerebral perfusion pressure = MAP − ICP. SAH: nimodipine routinely used (consider its own BP effects before adding antihypertensives).
sections.Management of Specific Conditions: Cardi: ACS: relieve pain first (nitroglycerin, morphine); IV nitroglycerin for sustained hypertension (coronary vasodilation + modest BP effect); labetalol as alternative (β-blockade + vasodilation); nitroprusside AVOIDED (coronary steal); pure vasodilators (diazoxide, hydralazine) avoided (reflex tachycardia → ↑O2 demand); mild-moderate HTN (<160/100) treated with oral β-blockers/ACE inhibitors. Acute LV dysfunction: diastolic dysfunction more common than systolic in hypertensive pulmonary oedema (preserved EF); labetalol excellent for diastolic dysfunction (↓HR improves filling time); nitroprusside effective in both systolic and diastolic dysfunction; diuretics for systolic dysfunction with volume overload, used CAUTIOUSLY in diastolic dysfunction (risk of over-diuresis/renal injury vs pulmonary oedema). Aortic dissection: Type A (ascending) = surgical; Type B (descending) = usually medical. Target SBP 100-110 mmHg, HR 60 bpm. Labetalol first-line (single agent, β+vasodilation). Vasodilator NEVER used alone (reflex tachycardia ↑dP/dt ↑shear force). Hydralazine CONTRAINDICATED. Verapamil/diltiazem = alternatives if β-blockers contraindicated.
sections.Management of Specific Conditions: Catec: Phaeochromocytoma: phentolamine (incremental doses/infusion) is drug of choice; β-blockers NEVER used without prior α-blockade (unopposed α-stimulation → paradoxical worsening); optimise intravascular volume (chronic vasoconstriction → relative hypovolaemia). MAOI-tyramine interaction: tyramine-rich foods (beer, Chianti wine, pickled fish/herring, avocado, liver) metabolised to octopamine (false neurotransmitter) → catecholamine release. Drug/alcohol-withdrawal hypertension often transient, responds to benzodiazepine sedation; if severe/sustained, labetalol (alternatives: nitroprusside, fenoldopam). Perioperative hypertension: incidence 30-80% in neurosurgical/vascular procedures (carotid endarterectomy, CABG, valve replacement, aortic/peripheral vascular surgery), up to 25% in noncardiac surgery. Assess and correct precipitating factors FIRST: pain, anxiety, hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, acidosis, hypervolaemia, bladder distension, hypothermia. Postoperative hypertension usually self-limited (2-6h) — favour short-acting titratable agents (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, labetalol, esmolol, nicardipine, urapidil, fenoldopam).

questions.question: A 62-year-old man with longstanding hypertension presents with severe headache, confusion and BP 228/138 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. CT head shows cerebral oedema without haemorrhage or infarction. Which of the following best describes his presentation and the appropriate initial management target?
options: A: Hypertensive emergency — reduce MAP by 20-25% over the first hours using parenteral agents, not to a normal range
B: Hypertensive urgency — mental status changes are expected with this degree of BP elevation and do not indicate emergency
C: Hypertensive urgency — lower BP over 24 hours with oral agents as no haemorrhage is present
D: Accelerated hypertension — hypertensive urgency category; papilloedema is not a criterion for emergency
E: Hypertensive emergency — immediately normalise BP to <120/80 mmHg to halt cerebral oedema
questions.explanation: This is a hypertensive emergency (hypertensive encephalopathy): severe hypertension with new progressive end-organ damage — neurological symptoms plus papilloedema indicate acute brain injury. Papilloedema specifically distinguishes emergency from accelerated hypertension (urgency), which has retinal haemorrhages/exudates WITHOUT papilloedema. The critical near-miss is normalising blood pressure to a standard target range: this is dangerous because the autoregulatory curve is shifted to higher pressures in chronic hypertensives — a 'normal' MAP may fall below their cerebral perfusion threshold causing ischaemia. Target is MAP reduction of 20-25% or DBP to 100-110 mmHg, not normalisation.
questions.topic: hypertensive-emergency urgency papilloedema encephalopathy MAP-reduction autoregulation

questions.question: A 58-year-old woman with chronic hypertension presents with a hypertensive emergency (BP 236/142 mmHg, rising creatinine, haematuria). What is the recommended initial blood pressure reduction target?
options: A: Reduce MAP by 20-25%, or reduce diastolic BP to 100-110 mmHg, over the first minutes to hours
B: Reduce MAP by 50% immediately to achieve a definitive therapeutic level
C: Reduce SBP to <120 mmHg within 60 minutes to prevent further renal injury
D: Normalise BP to 120/80 mmHg within 2 hours
E: Lower SBP to <150 mmHg over 24 hours using oral agents (hypertensive urgency target)
questions.explanation: The recommended initial target in hypertensive emergencies is a MAP reduction of 20-25%, or diastolic BP to 100-110 mmHg, over minutes to hours depending on the clinical scenario. Blood pressure is intentionally NOT lowered to a normal range because vascular autoregulatory mechanisms in chronically hypertensive patients are shifted to higher pressures; normalisation risks hypoperfusion of vital organs — stroke, myocardial ischaemia, mesenteric ischaemia, and renal deterioration. The near-miss — normalising to 120/80 mmHg — represents the most common and potentially harmful clinical error.
questions.topic: hypertensive-emergency MAP-reduction target autoregulation cerebral-perfusion renal

questions.question: A 54-year-old man presents with severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back. BP is 186/108 mmHg in the left arm and 149/92 mmHg in the right arm. CT angiography confirms type A aortic dissection. Cardiac surgery is arranged. What is the most appropriate immediate antihypertensive strategy?
options: A: IV hydralazine to rapidly reduce arterial pressure through direct arteriolar dilation
B: IV sodium nitroprusside alone for fast-acting titratable BP reduction
C: IV furosemide to reduce preload and systemic BP
D: IV labetalol titrated to SBP 100-110 mmHg and heart rate 60 bpm
E: IV nitroglycerin alone targeting SBP <120 mmHg
questions.explanation: In acute aortic dissection, the immediate goal is SBP ≤110 mmHg with HR 60 bpm. Labetalol — with combined β and α-blocking activity — is first-line as it reduces both HR and arterial pressure with a single agent. Crucially, a vasodilator (nitroglycerin, nitroprusside) must NEVER be used as monotherapy: vasodilation causes reflex tachycardia that increases the dPmax (rate of pressure rise) and worsens aortic shear force — the primary mechanism of dissection propagation. Hydralazine is specifically contraindicated as it increases HR, cardiac output and myocardial O2 consumption. If nitroprusside is added, β-blockade must already be established.
questions.topic: aortic-dissection labetalol shear-force SBP-target HR-target vasodilator-contraindicated

questions.question: A 47-year-old woman with a known adrenal mass presents in hypertensive crisis (BP 255/148 mmHg) with episodic headache, diaphoresis and palpitations. Urinary catecholamines are markedly elevated confirming phaeochromocytoma. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: A: Oral clonidine to reduce central sympathetic outflow
B: IV phentolamine in incremental doses
C: IV labetalol — α and β combined blockade is ideal for catecholamine excess
D: IV sodium nitroprusside as a rapid vasodilator
E: IV esmolol to control the tachycardia and reduce myocardial oxygen demand
questions.explanation: Phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis is managed with phentolamine (peripheral α-adrenergic blocker). β-blockers — including esmolol and labetalol — are CONTRAINDICATED in the acute phase: blocking β2-mediated vasodilation leaves unopposed α-adrenergic stimulation of peripheral vasculature, potentially causing a paradoxical severe worsening of hypertension and peripheral vasoconstriction. Labetalol is the classic near-miss: although it has α-blocking activity, its IV formulation is predominantly a β-blocker (7:1 β:α ratio), making it unsuitable in acute phaeochromocytoma. After adequate α-blockade is established, β-blockade can be added for rate control.
questions.section: Management of Specific Conditions: Catecholamine Excess and Perioperative
questions.topic: phaeochromocytoma phentolamine alpha-blocker beta-blocker-contraindicated catecholamine-crisis

questions.question: A 72-year-old man presents with acute left-sided weakness and aphasia. CT confirms ischaemic stroke. He is within the thrombolysis window with no contraindications. His admission BP is 202/108 mmHg. What is the blood pressure target required before and during thrombolytic therapy?
options: A: SBP <220 mmHg, DBP <120 mmHg (treat only extreme elevations — applicable to non-thrombolysis patients)
B: Normalise BP to <140/90 mmHg before administering alteplase
C: SBP <185 mmHg and DBP <110 mmHg
D: MAP reduction by 20-25% from admission value using IV labetalol
E: No antihypertensive therapy — elevated BP is a necessary compensatory response to maintain penumbral perfusion
questions.explanation: For ischaemic stroke patients eligible for thrombolysis, the guideline target is SBP <185 mmHg and DBP <110 mmHg before and during thrombolysis — necessary to reduce haemorrhagic transformation risk. IV labetalol or nicardipine are the recommended agents. The SBP >220 mmHg or DBP >120 mmHg threshold is the correct target for ischaemic stroke patients NOT eligible for thrombolysis — the near-miss confusing thrombolysis-eligible vs ineligible patients. Normalising BP risks worsening the ischaemic penumbra.
questions.topic: ischaemic-stroke thrombolysis BP-target SBP-185 labetalol nicardipine penumbra

questions.question: A patient with renal impairment has been receiving sodium nitroprusside at 4.5 mcg/kg/min for 96 hours. Despite no change in infusion rate, they develop progressive lactic acidosis, confusion and haemodynamic deterioration. What is the most likely diagnosis and the correct initial treatment?
options: A: Thiocyanate toxicity — institute haemodialysis to remove thiocyanate
B: Hypertensive rebound from tachyphylaxis — increase nitroprusside dose
C: Nitroprusside-induced pulmonary shunting — reduce PEEP and FiO2
D: Methaemoglobinaemia — administer methylene blue
E: Cyanide toxicity — stop infusion, give 100% O2 and sodium thiosulphate 150 mg/kg IV
questions.explanation: Cyanide toxicity from nitroprusside presents with metabolic lactic acidosis, confusion and cardiovascular deterioration — particularly at infusion rates >3 mcg/kg/min for >72 hours, especially with renal or hepatic impairment (impaired rhodanase activity and thiocyanate excretion). Treatment: stop infusion, 100% O2, sodium thiosulphate 150 mg/kg IV (converts cyanide to thiocyanate), sodium nitrite, or hydroxycobalamin 5g IV. The near-miss — thiocyanate toxicity — presents differently: abdominal pain, delirium, headache, nausea and restlessness rather than haemodynamic collapse and lactic acidosis. Both conditions may coexist in renal failure.
questions.topic: nitroprusside cyanide-toxicity thiocyanate thiosulphate hydroxycobalamin lactic-acidosis renal-impairment

questions.question: A patient presents with hypertensive emergency (BP 218/132 mmHg) complicated by acute STEMI. Which antihypertensive agent should be AVOIDED due to the risk of intracoronary steal worsening myocardial ischaemia?
options: A: IV labetalol (β and α-blocker)
B: Oral aspirin and morphine for pain
C: IV esmolol (cardioselective β-blocker)
D: IV sodium nitroprusside
E: IV nitroglycerin (coronary and venous dilator)
questions.explanation: Sodium nitroprusside may increase mortality when infused in the early hours of acute MI by causing intracoronary steal — vasodilation of normal coronary vessels preferentially diverts blood away from ischaemic areas that depend on collateral flow. It is no longer used for this indication. IV nitroglycerin is the preferred agent in ACS as it dilates coronary arteries and reduces preload without the steal effect. Labetalol and esmolol are appropriate as they reduce myocardial O2 demand through β-blockade. Pure vasodilators (diazoxide, hydralazine) should also be avoided due to reflex tachycardia increasing O2 demand.
questions.topic: ACS hypertensive-emergency nitroprusside coronary-steal nitroglycerin avoided myocardial-ischaemia

questions.question: A 31-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with BP 182/118 mmHg, proteinuria and severe frontal headache. Severe preeclampsia is diagnosed. Which antihypertensive agent is most appropriate for acute management?
options: A: IV furosemide — diuresis is first-line as these patients are volume overloaded
B: IV labetalol
C: IV sodium nitroprusside for fast, titratable BP control
D: Sublingual nifedipine for immediate BP reduction
E: IV enalaprilat (ACE inhibitor) for rapid, renally protective BP reduction
questions.explanation: Labetalol is effective and safe in preeclampsia: it reduces BP while maintaining uteroplacental perfusion. Nicardipine is also described as effective and safe. ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal renal toxicity. Nitroprusside carries a risk of fetal cyanide toxicity and should be avoided. Sublingual nifedipine is unacceptable due to inability to control rate or magnitude of BP reduction, with serious adverse events described. Hydralazine remains a parenteral option (improves placental blood flow) but labetalol and calcium antagonists are now preferred due to fewer maternal and perinatal side effects. Patients with hypertensive crises typically have REDUCED — not expanded — circulating volume (pressure natriuresis), so diuretics have a limited role.
questions.topic: preeclampsia labetalol pregnancy ACE-inhibitor-contraindicated nifedipine-contraindicated nicardipine

questions.question: A 55-year-old with BP 252/158 mmHg presents with confusion, severe headache and papilloedema. CT shows cerebral oedema without haemorrhage. Hypertensive encephalopathy is diagnosed. The team plans to reduce MAP by 15% over 2 hours. Which agent should be avoided in this clinical context?
options: A: IV sodium nitroprusside
B: IV esmolol
C: IV labetalol
D: Oral clonidine
E: IV nicardipine
questions.explanation: Clonidine is specifically contraindicated in hypertensive encephalopathy: its sedative effects impair the ability to monitor mental status, which is both a key monitoring parameter and a diagnostic tool — improvement in mental status within hours of BP reduction is expected and diagnostic for hypertensive encephalopathy; failure to improve should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis. Labetalol and esmolol are specifically recommended; nicardipine may be used. The near-miss is nitroprusside — while it can increase ICP and is less ideal in intracranial pathology, it is not specifically listed as contraindicated in hypertensive encephalopathy (unlike in ICH or traumatic brain injury where it may worsen ICP).
questions.topic: hypertensive-encephalopathy clonidine avoid neuro-monitoring PRES labetalol esmolol

questions.question: A 69-year-old man with stage 4 CKD (eGFR 16 ml/min/1.73m²) develops a hypertensive emergency (BP 214/130 mmHg). The team considers antihypertensive agents. Which agent offers the specific advantage of renal vasodilation, natriuresis and absence of toxic metabolites, making it particularly suitable in this patient?
options: A: IV nitroglycerin, which improves renal blood flow through venodilation
B: IV hydralazine, which improves renal perfusion through direct arteriolar dilation
C: IV labetalol, which reduces SVR and renal vascular resistance
D: IV enalaprilat (IV ACE inhibitor), which is renally protective in hypertensive crisis
E: IV fenoldopam
questions.explanation: Fenoldopam is a short-acting peripheral postsynaptic dopamine-1 receptor agonist. Its antihypertensive action combines arterial vasodilation, renal vasodilation and natriuresis — particularly advantageous in renal impairment. Crucially, it has NO toxic metabolites, unlike nitroprusside where thiocyanate accumulates renally and cyanide accumulates in hepatic dysfunction. Fenoldopam and nitroprusside are equally effective antihypertensives overall, making fenoldopam the preferred alternative in renal failure. Enalaprilat should be used cautiously: it may precipitate acute renal failure in patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis and has a long, variable duration of action that is difficult to titrate. Fenoldopam's side effects include dose-related tachycardia and increased intraocular pressure.
questions.topic: fenoldopam dopamine-1 renal-vasodilation natriuresis CKD no-toxic-metabolites nitroprusside-alternative

questions.question: In the management of acute type B aortic dissection, which of the following antihypertensive approaches is CONTRAINDICATED?
options: A: Adding IV nitroprusside after β-blockade is established
B: IV hydralazine for rapid arterial pressure reduction
C: Targeting SBP 100-110 mmHg with concurrent opioid analgesia
D: Substituting verapamil for β-blockade when β-blockers are contraindicated
E: IV labetalol as first-line combined β and α-blocker
questions.explanation: Hydralazine is specifically contraindicated in aortic dissection: it is a direct arteriolar dilator that increases heart rate, cardiac output and myocardial oxygen consumption, thereby increasing the rate of aortic pressure change (dP/dt) and worsening aortic shear force — the primary driver of dissection propagation. The general rule is that a vasodilator alone (without β-blockade) must NEVER be used in dissection for the same reason. Nitroprusside is acceptable when added AFTER β-blockade is established. Labetalol is first-line as a single agent. Verapamil or diltiazem are suitable alternatives if β-blockers are contraindicated.
questions.topic: aortic-dissection hydralazine-contraindicated shear-force labetalol vasodilator-alone

questions.question: A junior doctor proposes administering IV furosemide 80 mg to reduce BP in a patient presenting with a hypertensive emergency (BP 228/138 mmHg) and no signs of pulmonary oedema or peripheral oedema. Which of the following best characterises the intravascular volume status in hypertensive crises and the role of diuretics?
options: A: Volume status is euvolaemic in all hypertensive crises; treatment should target SVR reduction exclusively
B: Volume status is unpredictable and a pulmonary artery catheter is required before any therapy
C: Patients typically have a REDUCED circulating volume from pressure natriuresis; diuretics have a limited role
D: Patients are typically volume-overloaded from sodium retention; furosemide is appropriate first-line therapy
E: Diuretics are first-line agents in all hypertensive emergencies due to their rapid onset of action
questions.explanation: Hypertensive crises are characterised by markedly elevated SVR and most often a REDUCED circulating volume secondary to pressure natriuresis and pressure-induced diuresis. Administering diuretics in this context risks worsening volume depletion and end-organ hypoperfusion. Diuretics are reserved for patients with obvious fluid overload (e.g. pulmonary oedema with raised JVP, peripheral oedema). Parenteral antihypertensive agents targeting SVR (e.g. labetalol, nitroprusside, nicardipine) are the appropriate treatment. The near-miss here is assuming these patients are volume-expanded and would benefit from diuretics — a common but incorrect assumption about hypertensive crises.
questions.topic: hypertensive-crisis intravascular-volume pressure-natriuresis diuretics SVR limited-role

questions.question: A 65-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 38% predicted) and stage 2 hypertension presents with a hypertensive emergency. The team considers IV labetalol. Which of the following is the most clinically relevant contraindication to labetalol in this patient?
options: A: Non-selective β-blockade may precipitate bronchospasm in obstructive pulmonary disease
B: Labetalol causes reflex tachycardia that worsens myocardial oxygen demand in COPD patients
C: Labetalol has no oral formulation, preventing transition to maintenance therapy
D: Labetalol is only effective in catecholamine-mediated hypertensive crises
E: Labetalol causes excessive bradycardia that is poorly tolerated haemodynamically in COPD
questions.explanation: Labetalol is a non-selective β-adrenergic blocker (with additional α-blocking activity); non-selective β-blockade antagonises β2-receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, potentially precipitating bronchospasm — it is specifically contraindicated in obstructive pulmonary diseases and 2nd/3rd degree heart block. The near-miss — attributing reflex tachycardia to labetalol — is incorrect: reflex tachycardia is a property of pure vasodilators and dihydropyridines, not labetalol, which typically maintains or mildly reduces HR. The claim that labetalol lacks an oral formulation is also false — it does have one. Nicardipine, fenoldopam or urapidil would be preferred alternatives in this COPD patient.
questions.topic: labetalol COPD bronchospasm non-selective-beta-blocker contraindication alternatives

questions.question: A patient on sodium nitroprusside infusion requires monitoring for cyanide toxicity. Which of the following does NOT increase the risk of cyanide toxicity from sodium nitroprusside?
options: A: Infusion rates exceeding 3 mcg/kg/min for more than 72 hours
B: Renal insufficiency (impaired thiocyanate excretion leading to accumulation)
C: Running the maximum infusion rate of 10 mcg/kg/min for extended periods
D: Hepatic insufficiency (impaired rhodanase activity reducing cyanide-to-thiocyanate conversion)
E: Co-administration of 100% oxygen
questions.explanation: 100% oxygen is the TREATMENT for cyanide toxicity — not a risk factor. It is administered alongside sodium thiosulphate (and optionally sodium nitrite or hydroxycobalamin) when cyanide toxicity is suspected. All other options increase risk: hepatic impairment reduces rhodanase-mediated cyanide detoxification; renal impairment impairs thiocyanate excretion leading to accumulation; prolonged high-dose infusion (>3 mcg/kg/min for >72h) overwhelms the detoxification pathway; and the maximum rate of 10 mcg/kg/min should NOT be maintained for more than 10 minutes. Monitoring for cyanide toxicity (lactic acidosis, confusion, haemodynamic deterioration) is mandatory in high-risk patients.
questions.topic: nitroprusside cyanide-toxicity risk-factors rhodanase thiocyanate oxygen-treatment monitoring

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the management of hypertensive urgency is INCORRECT?
options: A: Asymptomatic severe hypertension does not require immediate blood pressure reduction
B: Most patients with hypertensive urgency do not require ICU admission
C: Immediate aggressive parenteral BP reduction in hypertensive urgency improves outcomes and reduces end-organ progression
D: Oral antihypertensive therapy is appropriate; blood pressure can be lowered over hours to 24 hours
E: Hypertensive urgency is severe hypertension without new or progressive end-organ injury
questions.explanation: There is NO evidence that immediate aggressive blood pressure lowering improves outcomes in hypertensive urgency — and considerable adverse effects can be precipitated by inappropriate rapid reductions, including stroke, myocardial ischaemia and renal deterioration. Oral therapy over hours to 24 hours is the appropriate strategy. The decision on whether to treat in hospital or as an outpatient should consider comorbidities and follow-up availability. All other statements are correct: the definition, the oral therapy approach, the lack of ICU requirement for most patients, and the fact that asymptomatic severe hypertension without end-organ injury does not warrant immediate BP reduction.
questions.topic: hypertensive-urgency no-evidence aggressive-lowering oral-therapy outpatient asymptomatic

questions.question: Regarding the classification and clinical features of hypertensive emergencies and urgencies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Accelerated hypertension — characterised by retinal haemorrhages and exudates WITH papilloedema — is classified as a hypertensive urgency

statements.label: B
statements.text: The degree of blood pressure elevation is the most important factor determining whether a patient has a hypertensive emergency versus urgency

statements.label: C
statements.text: A hypertensive emergency is defined by new or progressive end-organ injury (not merely the absolute blood pressure level) in the setting of severe hypertension

statements.label: D
statements.text: The rate of blood pressure rise and the patient's prior baseline blood pressure are more important determinants of end-organ injury than the absolute blood pressure level

questions.topic: hypertensive-emergency urgency definition end-organ-damage autoregulation rate-of-rise
questions.explanation: Classifying accelerated hypertension (urgency) as presenting WITH papilloedema is false — accelerated hypertension is characterised by retinal exudates and haemorrhages WITHOUT papilloedema; papilloedema is exactly what distinguishes hypertensive encephalopathy (an emergency) from accelerated hypertension. Treating the degree of blood pressure elevation as the most important factor determining emergency versus urgency is false — a previously normotensive patient may develop end-organ injury at a diastolic pressure of only 100–110 mmHg, while a chronic hypertensive may remain asymptomatic above 120 mmHg diastolic. Defining a hypertensive emergency by new or progressive end-organ injury rather than the absolute blood pressure level is true — end-organ damage is the defining feature. Rating the rate of blood pressure rise and the patient's prior baseline as more important determinants of end-organ injury than the absolute level is also true, and explains why apparently 'mild' elevations can precipitate emergencies in previously normotensive individuals.

questions.question: Regarding sodium nitroprusside and its toxicity:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Cyanide toxicity from nitroprusside typically presents with metabolic lactic acidosis, confusion and haemodynamic deterioration

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sodium thiosulphate 15 mg/kg IV converts cyanide to thiocyanate and is a key treatment for nitroprusside-induced cyanide toxicity

statements.label: C
statements.text: Sodium nitroprusside remains first-line in acute myocardial infarction because its rapid onset outweighs any theoretical coronary steal risk

statements.label: D
statements.text: Thiocyanate toxicity typically presents with lactic acidosis and cardiovascular collapse, similar to cyanide toxicity

questions.topic: nitroprusside cyanide thiocyanate lactic-acidosis coronary-steal thiosulphate toxicity
questions.explanation: Describing cyanide toxicity as presenting with metabolic lactic acidosis, confusion and cardiovascular deterioration is true — these are the 'hard' signs requiring immediate intervention. Citing a sodium thiosulphate dose of 15 mg/kg IV as the treatment is false — the correct dose is 150 mg/kg IV; a 10-fold underdose would be inadequate to convert accumulating cyanide to thiocyanate. Claiming nitroprusside remains first-line in acute MI is false — it is no longer used there because of coronary steal risk, with nitroglycerin preferred instead. Describing thiocyanate toxicity as presenting similarly to cyanide toxicity (lactic acidosis, cardiovascular collapse) is false — thiocyanate toxicity instead causes abdominal pain, delirium, headache, nausea, muscle spasms and restlessness.

questions.question: Regarding labetalol in hypertensive emergencies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Labetalol is contraindicated in patients with obstructive pulmonary disease and second or third degree heart block

statements.label: B
statements.text: Labetalol is effective and safe in preeclampsia/eclampsia, maintaining uteroplacental perfusion

statements.label: C
statements.text: Intravenous labetalol has a β to α blocking ratio of 1:7 (predominantly α-blocking), reducing systemic vascular resistance while causing minimal changes in heart rate and cardiac output

statements.label: D
statements.text: Labetalol is the drug of choice for acute phaeochromocytoma crisis because its combined α and β blockade controls catecholamine excess

questions.topic: labetalol beta-alpha-ratio preeclampsia phaeochromocytoma-contraindicated COPD heart-block
questions.explanation: Labetalol being contraindicated in obstructive pulmonary disease and second- or third-degree heart block is true — non-selective β2-blockade can cause bronchospasm in obstructive airways disease, and heart block is a standard β-blocker contraindication. Labetalol being effective and safe in preeclampsia, maintaining uteroplacental perfusion, is true — labetalol and calcium antagonists are now preferred over hydralazine in this setting. Describing the IV formulation's β:α blocking ratio as 1:7 (predominantly α-blocking) is false — the actual ratio is 7:1 (predominantly β-blocking), which is exactly why labetalol is unsuitable as monotherapy in phaeochromocytoma, where unopposed α-stimulation must be avoided. Calling labetalol the drug of choice for acute phaeochromocytoma crisis is false — it is CONTRAINDICATED there, since its predominantly β-blocking action leaves α-adrenergic stimulation unopposed, risking paradoxical severe hypertension; phentolamine is the actual treatment of choice.

questions.question: Regarding blood pressure management in acute intracranial events:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In ischaemic stroke NOT eligible for thrombolysis, antihypertensive treatment is recommended only for SBP >160 mmHg or DBP >90 mmHg

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sodium nitroprusside and nitroglycerin are preferred antihypertensive agents in elevated intracranial pressure because they rapidly reduce MAP without affecting ICP

statements.label: C
statements.text: Nimodipine is routinely used in subarachnoid haemorrhage and its blood pressure effects must be considered before adding further antihypertensives

statements.label: D
statements.text: In ischaemic stroke eligible for thrombolysis, the target blood pressure is SBP <185 mmHg and DBP <110 mmHg

questions.topic: stroke thrombolysis BP-target ICP nitroprusside-ICP labetalol nimodipine SAH
questions.explanation: Setting the treatment threshold for non-thrombolysis-eligible ischaemic stroke at SBP >160 mmHg or DBP >90 mmHg is false — the correct threshold is much higher, SBP >220 mmHg or DBP >120 mmHg, reflecting that elevated BP is partly compensatory for raised intracranial pressure in this group. Describing nitroprusside and nitroglycerin as preferred agents in elevated intracranial pressure is false — both can INCREASE cerebral blood volume and intracranial pressure while lowering MAP, potentially worsening cerebral perfusion pressure; labetalol is preferred instead as it does not appear to raise ICP. Nimodipine being routinely used in subarachnoid haemorrhage, with its BP-lowering effects needing consideration before adding further antihypertensives, is true. The thrombolysis-eligible stroke target of SBP <185 mmHg and DBP <110 mmHg is also true — this threshold prevents haemorrhagic transformation, with labetalol and nicardipine as the recommended agents.

questions.question: Regarding acute aortic dissection management:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The immediate haemodynamic goal is SBP ≤110 mmHg with heart rate ≤60 bpm to minimise aortic wall shear force

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hydralazine is an acceptable agent for acute aortic dissection as it provides rapid arterial pressure reduction without affecting heart rate

statements.label: C
statements.text: A vasodilator agent should NEVER be used as monotherapy in aortic dissection as it causes reflex tachycardia that increases dP/dt and shear force

statements.label: D
statements.text: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine or amlodipine are appropriate alternatives when β-blockers are contraindicated in aortic dissection

questions.topic: aortic-dissection SBP-target HR-target hydralazine-contraindicated vasodilator-alone verapamil
questions.explanation: Targeting SBP ≤110 mmHg with heart rate ≤60 bpm in acute aortic dissection is true — these dual targets address both pressure and rate, the key determinants of aortic wall stress. Describing hydralazine as an acceptable agent for acute dissection is false — it is specifically CONTRAINDICATED, since it markedly increases heart rate, cardiac output and myocardial oxygen demand, worsening the shear forces that propagate dissection. The principle that a vasodilator should never be used as monotherapy in dissection, because reflex tachycardia increases dP/dt and shear force, is true — this is exactly why labetalol (combined β- and α-blockade) is the ideal single agent. Recommending dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine or amlodipine as appropriate alternatives when β-blockers are contraindicated is false — it is the non-dihydropyridine, rate-limiting agents verapamil and diltiazem that provide both rate control and vasodilation; dihydropyridines lack the rate-control component and can themselves trigger reflex tachycardia.

questions.question: Regarding specific pharmacological agents used in hypertensive emergencies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fast-acting sublingual nifedipine is an acceptable option for rapid blood pressure reduction in hypertensive urgencies

statements.label: B
statements.text: Enalaprilat (IV ACE inhibitor) is contraindicated in pregnancy

statements.label: C
statements.text: Fenoldopam is a dopamine-1 receptor agonist that combines arterial vasodilation with renal vasodilation and natriuresis and has no toxic metabolites

statements.label: D
statements.text: Urapidil is a peripheral β-blocker with additional central dopamine receptor agonist activity that prevents reflex tachycardia

questions.topic: fenoldopam urapidil enalaprilat nifedipine sublingual-unacceptable ACE-pregnancy
questions.explanation: Describing fast-acting sublingual nifedipine as an acceptable option for rapid BP reduction in hypertensive urgencies is false — it is UNACCEPTABLE: the rate and magnitude of pressure reduction cannot be controlled, and serious adverse effects including death have been described. Enalaprilat (IV ACE inhibitor) being contraindicated in pregnancy is true — all ACE inhibitors carry this contraindication due to fetal renal toxicity and other teratogenic effects. Describing fenoldopam as a dopamine-1 receptor agonist combining arterial and renal vasodilation with natriuresis and no toxic metabolites is true. Describing urapidil as a peripheral β-blocker with additional central dopamine-receptor agonist activity is false — it is actually a peripheral α1-adrenergic BLOCKER with central 5-HT1A (serotonin, not dopamine) receptor agonism; this central action reduces sympathetic outflow and suppresses the reflex tachycardia that would otherwise follow peripheral α1-blockade.

questions.question: Regarding hypertensive crises due to catecholamine excess:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Phentolamine (α-adrenergic blocker) is the drug of choice for acute phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis

statements.label: B
statements.text: Foods containing tyramine (e.g. aged cheese, Chianti wine, pickled herring, avocado) can precipitate hypertensive crisis in patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors

statements.label: C
statements.text: In phaeochromocytoma crisis, β-blockers must not be used without prior α-blockade as they can cause paradoxical worsening of hypertension through unopposed α-adrenergic stimulation

statements.label: D
statements.text: Clonidine is absolutely contraindicated in all catecholamine-excess states as it paradoxically increases catecholamine release

questions.section: Management of Specific Conditions: Catecholamine Excess and Perioperative
questions.topic: phaeochromocytoma phentolamine MAO-inhibitor tyramine clonidine catecholamine-excess
questions.explanation: Phentolamine being the drug of choice for acute phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis is true — it is the specific treatment of choice here and in other catecholamine-excess emergencies, including MAOI interactions and antihypertensive withdrawal. Tyramine-containing foods (aged cheese, Chianti, pickled herring, avocado) being able to precipitate hypertensive crisis in patients on MAO inhibitors is true — tyramine, normally metabolised by MAO, is instead converted to octopamine, a false neurotransmitter that releases catecholamines. The principle that β-blockers must not be used without prior α-blockade in phaeochromocytoma crisis, since they can paradoxically worsen hypertension via unopposed α-adrenergic stimulation, is true. Describing clonidine as absolutely contraindicated in all catecholamine-excess states because it paradoxically increases catecholamine release is false — as a central α2-agonist it reduces sympathetic outflow and is not contraindicated across the board; it is specifically useful in clonidine-withdrawal and some antihypertensive-withdrawal states, and should be avoided only where its sedative effect would interfere with neurological assessment.

questions.question: Regarding the management of acute left ventricular dysfunction in the setting of hypertensive emergency:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Labetalol is particularly effective in hypertensive emergency with diastolic dysfunction as the reduction in heart rate facilitates LV filling

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hypertensive pulmonary oedema is more commonly due to diastolic dysfunction than systolic dysfunction, with most patients having preserved LV ejection fraction

statements.label: C
statements.text: Nitroprusside is effective only in systolic LV dysfunction and should be avoided in diastolic dysfunction due to excessive preload reduction

statements.label: D
statements.text: Diuretics are safe and should be administered aggressively early in all patients with hypertensive emergency and pulmonary oedema to reduce filling pressures

questions.topic: LV-dysfunction diastolic systolic pulmonary-oedema labetalol diuretics nitroprusside preserved-EF
questions.explanation: Labetalol being particularly effective in hypertensive emergency with diastolic dysfunction, since reducing heart rate facilitates LV filling, is true — more diastolic filling time directly addresses the mechanism of diastolic dysfunction. Hypertensive pulmonary oedema being more commonly due to diastolic than systolic dysfunction, with most patients having preserved ejection fraction, is true — the hypertrophied, non-compliant LV fails to fill adequately at the acutely elevated pressure. Restricting nitroprusside to systolic LV dysfunction and avoiding it in diastolic dysfunction due to excessive preload reduction is false — as both an arterial and venous dilator it is effective regardless of systolic or diastolic aetiology. Recommending diuretics be given safely and aggressively early in all patients with hypertensive emergency and pulmonary oedema is false — in diastolic dysfunction they should be used CAUTIOUSLY, since aggressive diuresis can excessively reduce the filling pressures the non-compliant ventricle needs, risking a swing from pulmonary oedema to renal failure.

questions.question: Regarding blood pressure measurement in clinical practice:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Blood pressure cuff width should be approximately 80% of the arm circumference, with the cuff length encircling 40% of the arm

statements.label: B
statements.text: Automated oscillometric blood pressure devices are equally reliable in haemodynamically unstable patients and in patients with arrhythmias as in normotensive patients in sinus rhythm

statements.label: C
statements.text: Systolic pressure corresponds to phase 5 Korotkoff sounds (disappearance of sounds) and diastolic to phase 1 (first appearance)

statements.label: D
statements.text: A cuff that is too small for the patient's arm circumference produces a falsely elevated blood pressure reading

questions.topic: BP-measurement Korotkoff cuff-size oscillometric intra-arterial-monitoring arrhythmia
questions.explanation: Specifying cuff width as approximately 80% of arm circumference and length as 40% is false — the dimensions are reversed: correct sizing is width 40% and length 80% of arm circumference, and incorrect proportions produce inaccurate readings. Describing automated oscillometric devices as equally reliable in haemodynamically unstable or arrhythmic patients as in normotensive sinus-rhythm patients is false — these devices are specifically LESS accurate in common ICU situations such as shock, arrhythmias and mechanical ventilation, and intra-arterial monitoring should be considered when potent antihypertensives are required. Assigning systolic pressure to Korotkoff phase 5 (disappearance) and diastolic to phase 1 (first appearance) is false — it is the reverse: phase 1 corresponds to systolic and phase 5 to diastolic. An undersized cuff producing a falsely elevated reading is true — this is the most common error in manual BP measurement.

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology and cerebral autoregulation in hypertensive emergencies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Acute increase in systemic vascular resistance — mediated by catecholamines, α-adrenergic activity and RAAS activation — is the most common pathophysiological mechanism in hypertensive crises

statements.label: B
statements.text: The cerebral autoregulatory curve is shifted to higher MAP values in patients with chronic hypertension, meaning a 'normal' MAP may be below their threshold for adequate cerebral perfusion

statements.label: C
statements.text: Once elevated, blood pressure should always be treated aggressively regardless of the clinical context, as persistence of hypertension invariably worsens neurological outcomes in stroke

statements.label: D
statements.text: Previously normotensive patients (e.g. acute glomerulonephritis, cocaine-induced, preeclampsia) only develop end-organ injury once diastolic blood pressure exceeds 150 mmHg, similar to chronically hypertensive patients

questions.topic: cerebral-autoregulation SVR RAAS normotensive preeclampsia stroke penumbra autoregulatory-shift
questions.explanation: Identifying an acute increase in systemic vascular resistance — mediated by catecholamines, α-adrenergic activity and RAAS activation — as the most common mechanism in hypertensive crises is true, with fluid overload a less common contributor. The cerebral autoregulatory curve being shifted to higher MAP values in chronic hypertension, so that a 'normal' MAP may fall below the threshold for adequate cerebral perfusion, is true — this rightward shift is exactly why BP should not be normalised acutely in hypertensive emergencies. Claiming BP should always be treated aggressively regardless of clinical context, since persistent hypertension invariably worsens neurological outcomes in stroke, is a dangerous overstatement and false — elevated BP in ischaemic stroke is partly compensatory for raised ICP, aggressive universal lowering can worsen the penumbra, and specific (not universal) targets apply. Claiming previously normotensive patients only develop end-organ injury above DBP 150 mmHg, similar to chronically hypertensive patients, is false — they have a normal, non-shifted autoregulatory curve and develop injury at the much lower threshold of DBP 100–110 mmHg.

questions.question: A 68-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with sudden collapse and reduced consciousness. BP is 210/126 mmHg. A non-contrast CT head is obtained (see figure). Based on the typical distribution of hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage, which anatomical region is most characteristically affected, as illustrated here?
options: A: Lobar (cortical-subcortical) haemorrhage, the pattern most typical of cerebral amyloid angiopathy rather than hypertension
B: Basal ganglia/thalamic region — the classic site for hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage
C: Isolated cerebellar haemorrhage without supratentorial extension
D: Extradural haematoma from head trauma
E: Isolated subarachnoid haemorrhage from aneurysmal rupture
questions.explanation: Hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage classically occurs in the basal ganglia/thalamic region (as shown here) and the cerebellum, reflecting rupture of small perforating arteries (e.g. lenticulostriate vessels) chronically weakened by hypertensive vasculopathy (Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms/lipohyalinosis). This contrasts with lobar haemorrhage, which is more typically associated with cerebral amyloid angiopathy, particularly in older normotensive or mildly hypertensive patients.
questions.topic: intracerebral haemorrhage basal ganglia hypertensive bleed CT imaging location

questions.question: The figure shows cerebral blood flow autoregulation curves for normotensive individuals and patients with chronic hypertension. A chronically hypertensive patient in a hypertensive emergency has their MAP acutely reduced from 180 mmHg to 90 mmHg — a level that would be entirely well-tolerated in a normotensive individual. Based on the curves shown, what is the most likely physiological consequence?
options: A: Cerebral hyperperfusion and rising intracranial pressure, since 90 mmHg still exceeds the lower autoregulatory threshold in this patient
B: The autoregulatory curve for chronic hypertension is identical to the normotensive curve once blood pressure has been elevated for more than a few weeks
C: No change in cerebral blood flow is possible outside the initial measured pressure range shown on the curve

D: Cerebral ischaemia, because 90 mmHg falls below the rightward-shifted lower limit of autoregulation in chronic hypertension, even though it would be within the plateau for a normotensive individual
E: No adverse effect, since 90 mmHg lies within the autoregulatory plateau for all individuals regardless of chronic BP history
questions.explanation: The autoregulatory curve is shifted rightward (to higher pressures) in chronic hypertension. A MAP of 90 mmHg falls within the flat autoregulatory plateau for a normotensive individual, maintaining stable cerebral blood flow — but the same 90 mmHg lies below the rightward-shifted lower limit of autoregulation in the chronically hypertensive patient, where cerebral blood flow becomes pressure-dependent and falls, risking cerebral ischaemia. This is precisely why hypertensive emergencies are managed by reducing MAP by only 20-25% rather than normalising blood pressure.
questions.topic: cerebral autoregulation curve shift chronic hypertension MAP reduction ischaemia

questions.question: A 58-year-old man presents with acute severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back, BP 195/115 mmHg, and a pulse deficit between the two arms. The figure shows a cardiac MRI obtained to confirm the diagnosis. Based on the segment of the aorta involved in the dissection shown, which management pathway is indicated?
options: A: Type A dissection — since it involves the ascending aorta, medical management alone is preferred to avoid surgical risk
B: The imaging finding is non-specific for dissection type and further invasive angiography is mandatory before any treatment decision can be made
C: Type A dissection — urgent surgical repair is required, with medical BP/HR control as a bridge to theatre
D: Type B dissection — medical management with a target SBP 100-110 mmHg and HR 60 bpm is definitive therapy
E: The ascending aorta involvement makes this a Type B dissection requiring endovascular stenting
questions.explanation: The MRI demonstrates an intimal flap involving the ascending aorta — a Type A dissection by the Stanford classification. Type A (proximal) dissections require urgent surgical repair because of the high risk of complications including aortic rupture, cardiac tamponade, and acute aortic regurgitation; medical therapy (β-blockade ± vasodilator, targeting SBP 100-110 mmHg and HR 60 bpm) is instituted immediately as a bridge to theatre, not as definitive treatment. Type B (distal, descending aorta only) dissections are usually managed medically unless complicated.
questions.topic: aortic dissection type A ascending aorta MRI imaging surgical management Stanford classification

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT among the recognised precipitating factors for perioperative hypertension that should be assessed and corrected before instituting antihypertensive drug therapy?
options: A: Postoperative pain
B: Bladder distension
C: Elevated haemoglobin
D: Hypercapnia
E: Hypothermia
questions.explanation: Recognised precipitating factors for perioperative hypertension include pain, anxiety, altered blood gases (hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, acidosis), hypervolaemia, bladder distension, and hypothermia — all of which should be assessed and corrected before considering antihypertensive drug therapy. Preoperative haemoglobin concentration is not among the specifically recognised precipitants in this context. Perioperative hypertension is most commonly associated with neurosurgical and vascular procedures (30-80% incidence) and can occur in up to 25% of noncardiac surgical patients; postoperative hypertension is usually self-limited (2-6 hours), favouring short-acting titratable agents.
questions.section: Management of Specific Conditions: Catecholamine Excess and Perioperative
questions.topic: perioperative hypertension precipitating factors pain bladder distension hypothermia incidence

questions.question: A 74-year-old woman with longstanding hypertension presents with acute pulmonary oedema and BP 210/122 mmHg. Chest radiograph shows pulmonary oedema without significant cardiomegaly. ECG shows voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Fundoscopy reveals flame haemorrhages and cotton wool spots. Which underlying mechanism of acute left ventricular dysfunction is most consistent with this presentation?
options: A: Diastolic dysfunction, from a non-compliant hypertrophied ventricle with impaired filling
B: Right ventricular failure secondary to acute pulmonary embolism
C: Systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction and ventricular dilatation
D: Acute papillary muscle rupture causing severe mitral regurgitation
E: High-output cardiac failure from a hyperdynamic circulation
questions.explanation: This presentation matches the recognised clinical pattern of diastolic (not systolic) LV dysfunction in hypertensive emergency: higher BP elevation, significant hypertensive retinopathy (flame haemorrhages, cotton wool spots), and LVH on ECG, WITHOUT cardiomegaly on CXR. The hypertrophied, non-compliant ventricle fails to fill adequately at the acutely elevated afterload, precipitating pulmonary oedema despite preserved (often normal) ejection fraction — the majority of hypertensive pulmonary oedema episodes are of this type. Systolic dysfunction, by contrast, typically presents with a lower BP elevation, absent retinopathy, and four-chamber cardiomegaly on CXR.
questions.topic: diastolic dysfunction systolic dysfunction hypertensive pulmonary oedema clinical clues LVH retinopathy
